summary: My name is Akamine Kosei, born in 1903 in Okinawa during the 38th year of the Meiji period. I grew up near Naha city in a farming family, and later came to Hawaii as a teenager before the Immigration Act of 1924 took effect. I traveled alone on the Siberia Maru ship and experienced the hard life of plantation work before attending school in Honolulu. Over time, I embraced Christianity and attended an Episcopal church-sponsored school.

I was born and raised in Okinawa Prefecture, specifically near what is now Naha city. Life there was poor and agricultural, with my family growing sugar and broomcorn. Okinawa was once prosperous before being governed by the Satsuma Domain and heavily taxed by the Japanese government starting from the Meiji period. I returned to Okinawa in 1971 with the Japanese Christian Association to visit churches and reconnect with my roots.

I lived in the suburbs of what is now Naha city before the war, in the Aza and Oroku areas. Naha was the capital city of Okinawa Prefecture where I spent my childhood. It was a place close to the ocean where we swam, fished, and hunted, and where I attended middle school before coming to Hawaii. The ship Siberia Maru left from Naha when I departed for Hawaii.

I was born in the 38th year of the Meiji period, which is 1903. The Meiji period marked when Okinawa became part of Japan, and during that time Okinawa was heavily taxed and oppressed by the Satsuma Domain. This era changed the economic and social conditions of Okinawa significantly, leading to hardships for my family and community.

I do not remember much about the Russo-Japanese War as I was very young at the time. It occurred around 1904-1905, but it did not leave a strong impression on me during my childhood in Okinawa. My memories from that period focus more on farming and family life.

I was not in Hawaii during the First World War, and I do not recall specific memories related to it from my childhood. My focus during those years was on farming life in Okinawa and later my immigration to Hawaii before the 1924 Immigration Act.

In 1971, I traveled back to Okinawa with the Japanese Christian Association to visit many churches across the islands, including places like Naka and Takejima. This trip was important to me as it connected my faith journey with my homeland. The association helped foster Christian ties between Hawaii and Okinawa.

I remember a lady evangelist in Okinawa who was originally born in Kyushu, Japan, but raised in Okinawa. Although I cannot recall her name, her background from Kyushu was notable as she contributed to Christian work in Okinawa. This shows the connections between different parts of Japan and Okinawa in religious efforts.

My parents immigrated to Hawaii when I was a toddler, working on sugar plantations cutting cane. I came to Hawaii myself at age 16 before the Immigration Act of 1924 closed the doors for many. Hawaii was very different from Okinawa, with warm weather year-round and a more open lifestyle. I worked on plantations, attended school sponsored by the Episcopal Church, and experienced the challenges and joys of immigrant life there.

My younger brother was sent by the University of Hawaii to an island in the South Pacific to study soil. This shows how my family was connected to educational opportunities beyond Hawaii and Okinawa. Unfortunately, my other brother remained in Okinawa and died during World War II.

The University of Hawaii played a role in my family as my younger brother was sent by the university to study soil on an island in the South Pacific. This connection to higher education was important for us, showing opportunities for advancement beyond plantation work. The university also represents a bridge between Hawaii and other Pacific regions.

I traveled alone to Hawaii on the ship called Siberia Maru. The journey took over ten days, departing from Naha at night and stopping at Kagoshima, Kobe, and Yokohama before heading straight to Hawaii. I experienced seasickness and loneliness on that long trip, and it was a significant moment in my life as I left Okinawa for a new world.

During my voyage to Hawaii on the Siberia Maru, the ship arrived in Kagoshima about the same time the next day after leaving Naha. This was one of the stops on the journey from Okinawa to Hawaii. I remember the timing but not much else about the stop itself.

After leaving Kagoshima, the Siberia Maru arrived in Kobe in the afternoon. A hotel clerk sent by Ebisuya, an Okinawan-owned hotel, met us there and we stayed for 10 days before continuing the journey. Kobe was an important transit point on my way to Hawaii.

Ebisuya was a hotel in Kobe owned by an Okinawan, and when I arrived there on the Siberia Maru, a clerk from Ebisuya met us. We stayed at this hotel for 10 days before continuing our journey to Hawaii. This hotel provided a brief respite during the long immigration trip.

The Siberia Maru stopped briefly in Yokohama in the afternoon, but we only had about two hours there and stayed on the ship. Yokohama was the last stop in Japan before the ship sailed directly to Hawaii. It was part of the long journey from Okinawa to my new home.

Okinawa became part of Japan during the Meiji period, but before that it was governed by the Satsuma Domain which oppressed the people. Japan had not started trading with China when Okinawa was prosperous, but after Satsuma's rule, Okinawa suffered heavy taxes and economic decline. My family’s experience reflects the broader historical relationship between Okinawa and Japan.

Before the Meiji period, Okinawa was governed by the Satsuma Domain, led by Lord Shimazu's government. This rule oppressed Okinawans and heavily taxed them, causing economic hardship and loss of prosperity. The people lost hope and incentive to work, and men turned to playing the Zamisen instrument instead of farming.

Before Japan began trading with China, Okinawa was already engaged in trade with China and was quite prosperous. This changed after the Satsuma Domain took control and Okinawa was incorporated into Japan during the Meiji period. The shift in trade and governance affected the economic conditions of my home.

After Okinawa was oppressed by the Satsuma Domain and heavily taxed, many men lost incentive to work and instead learned to play the Zamisen, an Okinawan string instrument. This cultural shift was part of how Okinawans coped with economic hardship during that time. It became a symbol of leisure as men played around rather than farmed.

My parents wanted me to come to Hawaii before the Immigration Act of 1924 was enacted because it would restrict Japanese immigration. I arrived in Hawaii at about age 16, traveling alone on the Siberia Maru just before the law took effect. This act changed the course of many families like mine by limiting immigration opportunities.

My parents were part of the Issei generation who came to Hawaii to work on sugar plantations. They worked in the fields for over ten years, living in plantation camps with many other Japanese immigrants. The Issei community formed the backbone of plantation labor during that era.

When I came to Hawaii, I attended an English school sponsored by the Episcopal Church. This church played a significant role in my education and introduction to Christianity. I began attending church services on Sundays and eventually embraced the Christian faith through their support.
The transcript does not mention Rev. Fukao, so I have no memories or experiences related to him.
The transcript does not mention Mackinley High School, so I have no memories or experiences related to this school.
